## 9. Some common pitfalls 
**Treating all FASTQ files as equivalent -**
A FASTQ from short-read SBS, HiFi, nanopore DNA, direct RNA, or single-cell RNA-seq can look superficially similar but represents very different experimental evidence.

**Ignoring library preparation -**
Many biases come before sequencing: extraction, fragmentation, PCR, size selection, barcoding, capture, and sample storage.

**Confusing read accuracy with assembly accuracy -**
A dataset with lower single-read accuracy can still produce a strong consensus if coverage and error models are handled correctly. Conversely, highly accurate reads can still produce poor assemblies if repeats, contamination, haplotypes, or coverage are mishandled.

**Forgetting reference bias -**
When reads are mapped to a single linear reference, sequences absent from that reference may be underdetected, mis-mapped, or lost. Pangenome graphs help, but only if the graph actually contains the relevant diversity.

**Missing metadata -**
Without sample, library, platform, chemistry, barcode, and pipeline metadata, future users cannot interpret or reproduce the analysis.

---
